title: Purchase Plus Improvements — Buy and Renovate with One Mortgage
metaDescription: Buy and renovate with one mortgage using Purchase Plus Improvements. Roll up to $40,000 (insured) of renovations into your mortgage at the same rate. BC explained.
intro: Buying a fixer-upper in Greater Vancouver? The Purchase Plus Improvements (PPI) program lets you roll renovation costs into your mortgage — at mortgage rates instead of credit-card or HELOC rates.

body.heading: How PPI works
paragraphs: You provide quotes for your planned renovations as part of the mortgage application. The lender uses the post-renovation appraised value to size the mortgage, then advances the renovation funds to a lawyer's trust account on closing. As renovations are completed, the funds release on confirmation by an inspection or invoice.

Insured PPI mortgages can include up to $40,000 of improvements (or 20% of the post-improvement value, whichever is less). Conventional/uninsured PPI can go higher. The renovations must be permanent and add value — kitchens, bathrooms, flooring, additions, suite conversions, landscaping. Cosmetic items like furniture aren't covered.

body.heading: Why PPI usually beats a separate renovation loan
paragraphs: Renovation costs financed through a HELOC sit at prime + 0.5% (around 6–7% in 2026). Through PPI they sit at your mortgage rate (often 4–5%). On a $40,000 renovation amortized over 25 years, that's a meaningful savings — and you avoid the appraisal and underwriting costs of a separate refinance later.

whoItsFor: Buying a property that needs immediate work
Want to add a legal suite, finish a basement or update kitchens/bathrooms
Are buying a home that's discounted because of its condition
requirements: Written quotes from licensed contractors for all proposed work
An appraisal that includes a post-improvement value estimate

faqs.question: Do I need to use specific contractors?
faqs.answer: Most lenders require licensed, bonded contractors with quotes on letterhead. You can usually do some work yourself, but the lender often won't release funds for DIY labour — only for materials and contracted work.

faqs.question: When are the renovation funds released?
faqs.answer: Typically after the work is completed and a final inspection or paid invoices are submitted. Some lenders allow staged releases mid-project. Plan to front the work or arrange short-term financing to bridge the gap.

faqs.question: Can I add a basement suite with PPI?
faqs.answer: Yes — and this is a popular Greater Vancouver use case. Adding a legal secondary suite often increases the property's appraised value by more than the cost of the work, and creates rental income that helps with future qualification.
